skill.name: agent-tools
skill.description: Run 250+ AI apps via inference.sh CLI - image generation, video creation, LLMs, search, 3D, Twitter automation. Models: FLUX, Veo, Gemini, Grok, Claude, Seedance, OmniHuman, Tavily, Exa, OpenRouter, and many more. Use when running AI apps, generating images/videos, calling LLMs, web search, or automating Twitter. Triggers: inference.sh, infsh, ai model, run ai, serverless ai, ai api, flux, veo, claude api, image generation, video generation, openrouter, tavily, exa search, twitter api, grok
skill.summary: Run 250+ AI apps via inference.sh CLI including FLUX, Veo, Claude, and more
skill.author: inference-sh-skills

security_audit.summary: This skill is a legitimate wrapper for the inference.sh CLI (belt). It documents how to install and use the CLI tool for accessing 250+ AI models and services. Static analysis flagged shell command patterns, but these are all false positives - the skill only contains documentation and example commands using the belt CLI. All external command invocations use hardcoded command names (belt app list, belt app run, etc.) with no user input injection vectors. Network access is for legitimate API calls to inference.sh services. The skill is safe for publication with appropriate user warnings about CLI usage.

medium_findings.title: CLI Command Documentation Pattern
medium_findings.description: The skill documents belt CLI commands extensively. Each bash code block containing `belt app list`, `belt app run`, `belt task get`, etc. is flagged by the static analyzer as an external command pattern. However, these are documentation examples showing how to use the CLI tool - not actual code execution risks. All command arguments are hardcoded strings, not user-controlled input.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: These are documentation examples showing CLI usage. The static analyzer detects pattern `belt app list` but cannot distinguish between actual code execution and documentation. Manual review confirms all instances are example commands in markdown code blocks.

medium_findings.title: URL Hardcoding in Documentation
medium_findings.description: The skill contains hardcoded URLs to inference.sh documentation and API endpoints. This is expected for a CLI tool wrapper skill - the URLs point to legitimate inference.sh infrastructure and are not security concerns.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: All hardcoded URLs point to inference.sh domains and documentation. This is standard documentation practice for a CLI wrapper skill with no malicious destination identified.

medium_findings.title: Shell Installer Pattern
medium_findings.description: Lines 16 and 44 of SKILL.md show the standard CLI installation pattern `curl -fsSL https://cli.inference.sh | sh`. This is a common pattern used by many legitimate CLI tools (Docker, kubectl, etc.). The static analyzer flags this as a pipe-to-shell blocker, but the installer is signed and verified with SHA-256 checksums as documented in the skill.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The installer is from a known legitimate source (inference.sh) and includes checksum verification. However, pipe-to-shell patterns should still warrant a warning to users who prefer manual installation.

low_findings.title: Hidden File Access Pattern
low_findings.description: Line 31 of SKILL.md mentions `~/.local/bin/inferencesh` which uses the home directory path. This is standard CLI installation documentation, not a security risk.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Home directory access here is for installing the CLI binary to a standard location. No sensitive file access or credential theft is indicated.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal Pattern in Documentation
low_findings.description: Line 41 of running-apps.md mentions relative paths like `./image.png` and `../data/video.mp4`. These are file path examples in documentation, not actual path traversal vulnerabilities.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: This is documentation showing supported path formats for the CLI tool. The CLI handles file uploads securely - this is not a path traversal vulnerability.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Pattern
low_findings.description: Line 163 of running-apps.md shows error message documentation. The static analyzer flags this as system reconnaissance, but this is simply error handling documentation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: This is error message documentation for common CLI errors. No actual system reconnaissance code exists.

dangerous_patterns.title: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm References
dangerous_patterns.description: Static analyzer flagged several instances of 'weak cryptographic algorithm'. Upon review, these appear to be false positives where the analyzer detected words like 'sha' in download URLs or command names. No actual weak cryptography is used - the skill only references SHA-256 checksums which is considered secure.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: SHA-256 checksums are used for file verification which is secure. The analyzer flagged 'sha256' string matches but actual weak crypto usage was not found.

content.user_title: Access 250+ AI Models via inference.sh CLI
content.value_statement: Running AI models locally requires expensive GPU hardware. This skill lets Claude access 250+ cloud AI models for image generation, video creation, LLM calls, and web search through a simple CLI.
actual_capabilities: Generate images with FLUX, Gemini 3 Pro, Grok Imagine, and other models
Create videos with Veo 3.1, Seedance, Wan, and OmniHuman avatar
Call Claude, Gemini, Kimi, and 250+ other LLMs via OpenRouter
Perform web search with Tavily and Exa search engines
Automate Twitter/X posts with post-tweet, media upload, and interactions
Generate 3D models, TTS audio, music, and other media outputs
limitations: Requires inference.sh account and API key for authentication
Usage may incur costs depending on model provider pricing
Local file uploads require proper path formatting and file accessibility

use_cases.title: On-demand Image and Video Generation
use_cases.description: Generate professional-quality images with FLUX or videos with Veo directly in conversations without leaving the terminal.
use_cases.target_user: Content creators, designers, and developers needing quick AI-generated media

use_cases.title: Multi-model LLM Comparison
use_cases.description: Compare responses across Claude, Gemini, Kimi, and other models by routing prompts through OpenRouter.
use_cases.target_user: AI researchers and developers evaluating model performance

use_cases.title: Automated Social Media Posting
use_cases.description: Post updates, images, and media to Twitter/X automatically from CI/CD pipelines or workflows.
use_cases.target_user: Social media managers and automation engineers

prompt_templates.title: Quick Image Generation
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the inference.sh CLI to generate an image of [description] using FLUX
prompt_templates.scenario: Basic image generation with default settings

prompt_templates.title: Video with Custom Prompt
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a [duration] video of [scene description] using Veo 3.1 through inference.sh

prompt_templates.title: Multi-step Media Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt:
1. Generate an image with FLUX showing [concept]
2. Convert it to video using Wan 2.5
3. Save both outputs to ./media/

prompt_templates.title: API-Integrated Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt:
1. Search for [topic] using Tavily
2. Summarize findings with Claude
3. Post summary to Twitter with a link to [source]

output_examples.input: belt app run falai/flux-dev-lora --input '{"prompt": "a sunset over mountains"}'
output_examples.output:
Running falai/flux-dev-lora
Task ID: abc123def456
Status: completed
Images: [url to generated image]

output_examples.input: belt app run google/veo-3-1-fast --input '{"prompt": "a drone shot over forest"}'
output_examples.output:
Running google/veo-3-1-fast
Task ID: ghi789jkl012
Status: completed
Videos: [url to generated video file]

output_examples.input: belt app run openrouter/claude-sonnet-45 --input '{"prompt": "Explain quantum entanglement"}'
output_examples.output:
Running openrouter/claude-sonnet-45
Task ID: mno345pqr678
Status: completed
Response: Quantum entanglement is a quantum mechanical phenomenon where...

best_practices: Use belt app sample before running to generate and review input schema
Store your INFSH_API_KEY in environment variables, never hardcode in scripts
Use --save flag to save outputs to files for easy access and version control
anti_patterns: Do not pipecurl directly to shell without reviewing the installer script first
Avoid running --no-wait without tracking the task ID for later retrieval
Do not assume all models support the same input schema - always check belt app get first

faq.question: Do I need to install the CLI separately?
faq.answer: Yes. Install with: curl -fsSL https://cli.inference.sh | sh. Manual install options are documented in SKILL.md.

faq.question: How does authentication work?
faq.answer: Run belt login for browser-based authentication, or set INFSH_API_KEY environment variable for scripts.

faq.question: What models are available?
faq.answer: 250+ models including FLUX, Veo, Claude, Gemini, Kimi, Tavily, Exa, and many more via OpenRouter.

faq.question: Are there usage costs?
faq.answer: Yes. Pricing depends on the model provider. Check inference.sh for current rates before heavy usage.

faq.question: Can I use local files?
faq.answer: Yes. Provide file paths instead of URLs and the CLI will upload them automatically.

faq.question: How do I track long-running tasks?
faq.answer: Use --no-wait to submit and get a task ID. Then check status with belt task get <task-id>.
